era_context: The year 1560 falls within the height of the European Renaissance and the early phase of the Counter-Reformation. The Ottoman Empire under Süleyman the Magnificent was consolidating its Mediterranean dominance. In Europe, religious conflict was intensifying between Catholics and Protestants. The Spanish Empire was expanding in the Americas and consolidating control in Europe. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty continued to govern China, Japan remained fragmented in the Sengoku period, and Korea was under Joseon rule.

events.title: Conspiracy of Amboise
events.description: The Conspiracy of Amboise was a failed Huguenot (French Protestant) plot to seize King Francis II and remove the powerful Guise family from power. The conspiracy, discovered in early 1560, resulted in the execution of numerous conspirators and intensified religious tensions in France. The plot demonstrated the growing power of Protestant nobles and the fragility of royal authority, setting the stage for the Wars of Religion that would dominate French politics for the next three decades.

sources.name: Contemporary French royal chronicles and court records
Historiographic consensus on French Wars of Religion

events.certainty_note: The conspiracy is extensively documented in contemporary French sources and is one of the best-attested events of the period.

events.title: Death of Gustav I Vasa
events.description: Gustav I Vasa, the founder of the modern Swedish state, died on 29 September 1560 at the age of 60. Gustav had ruled Sweden for 37 years, transforming it from a union under Danish dominance into an independent kingdom. His death marked the end of an era of consolidation and the beginning of succession disputes that would lead to the reign of his volatile son Erik XIV. Gustav's legacy included the establishment of a centralized Swedish state, the adoption of Protestantism, and the assertion of Swedish independence.

sources.name: Swedish royal chronicles and contemporary records

events.certainty_note: The date of Gustav I Vasa's death is precisely documented in Swedish royal records.

events.title: Battle of Diu
events.description: Portuguese naval forces under the command of the Viceroy of India defeated an Ottoman-Gujarati coalition fleet at Diu in 1560. The battle was a significant naval engagement in the Indian Ocean, demonstrating Portuguese naval superiority and consolidating Portuguese control over maritime trade routes in the region. The victory prevented Ottoman expansion into the Indian Ocean and secured Portuguese dominance in the Arabian Sea for decades.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Portuguese India and Ottoman expansion

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Diu in 1560 is well-documented in Portuguese colonial records and is a major event in the history of Portuguese India.
cross_references: Part of broader Portuguese-Ottoman rivalry in the Indian Ocean

events.title: Scottish Reformation Parliament
events.description: The Scottish Parliament, meeting in August 1560, voted to abolish papal authority in Scotland and adopt a Protestant confession of faith. This action, known as the Scottish Reformation, was driven by Protestant nobles and clergy and represented a dramatic shift in Scottish religious and political life. The reformation was formalized by the Parliament of 1560 and led to the establishment of the Church of Scotland as a Presbyterian institution, fundamentally altering the religious landscape of Britain.

sources.name: Scottish parliamentary records and contemporary chronicles
John Knox's writings and later accounts
Historiographic consensus on the Scottish Reformation

events.certainty_note: The Scottish Reformation Parliament of 1560 is extensively documented in parliamentary records and contemporary sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Protestant Reformation in Britain and Europe

events.title: Treaty of Edinburgh
events.description: The Treaty of Edinburgh, signed on 6 July 1560, ended French military intervention in Scotland and established English influence over Scottish affairs. The treaty was negotiated between England, Scotland, and France and resulted in the withdrawal of French troops from Scotland. The agreement effectively ended the 'Auld Alliance' between Scotland and France and marked a significant shift in British and European geopolitics, bringing Scotland closer to England and away from French influence.
key_figures: Mary, Queen of Scots (absent from negotiations)
events.certainty_note: The Treaty of Edinburgh is precisely dated to 6 July 1560 and is extensively documented in diplomatic records.

events.title: Accession of Charles IX of France
events.description: Charles IX became King of France on 5 December 1560 following the death of his brother Francis II. Charles was only ten years old at his accession, and real power was exercised by his mother, Catherine de Medici, who served as regent. Charles IX's reign would be marked by the Wars of Religion and the infamous St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre in 1572. His accession represented a continuation of Valois rule but under the dominant influence of Catherine de Medici.
key_figures: Catherine de Medici (Queen Mother and regent)

sources.name: French royal chronicles and court records

events.certainty_note: The accession of Charles IX is precisely dated to 5 December 1560 in French royal records.

events.title: Oda Nobunaga's rise to power in Japan
events.description: Oda Nobunaga, a daimyo (feudal lord) of Owari Province, began his rise to power in 1560 by defeating a rival daimyo at the Battle of Okehazama. This victory marked the beginning of Nobunaga's military campaigns to unify Japan, which would continue until his death in 1582. Nobunaga's rise represented a shift in Japanese politics from the fragmented Sengoku period toward centralization and the eventual unification of Japan under the Tokugawa shogunate.

sources.name: Japanese chronicles and records (Oda Nobunaga monogatari, Shinchōkoki)

events.certainty_note: The Battle of Okehazama is traditionally dated to 1560, though some sources place it in 1559. The exact date is debated among historians, but 1560 is the conventional dating.
cross_references: Part of broader Japanese unification process

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of the Battle of Okehazama in 1560 is sometimes exaggerated in popular accounts; while it was an important victory for Nobunaga, it did not immediately lead to his dominance—his rise to power was a gradual process spanning two decades. Additionally, some Japanese sources date the battle to 1559, creating uncertainty about the precise year. The Treaty of Edinburgh's significance in ending French influence in Scotland is sometimes overstated; French cultural and dynastic ties to Scotland persisted, and Mary, Queen of Scots' claim to the Scottish throne remained contested.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1560 is rich in Western European sources, particularly for France, Scotland, and England. Portuguese colonial records provide detailed accounts of the Battle of Diu. However, Japanese sources for this period are primarily later compilations (written in the 17th century or later), making precise dating of events like the Battle of Okehazama uncertain. Ottoman and Islamic sources for this period exist but are less commonly consulted in Western historiography. The record is heavily shaped by European and East Asian elite perspectives; indigenous and non-elite voices are largely absent.

graph_edges.note: The Conspiracy of Amboise prompted the issuance of the Edict of Amboise in 1561.
Gustav I Vasa's death led directly to the coronation of Erik XIV in 1561.
The Scottish Reformation Parliament and the Treaty of Edinburgh occurred in the same year and were closely related.
Charles IX's accession and regency under Catherine de Medici set the stage for the St. Bartholomew's Day Massacre in 1572.
Nobunaga's early victories in 1560 led to his entry into Kyoto and consolidation of power in 1568.
